coredata.dc:title: Advanced g-C3N4 and bimetallic FeNi-BTC integration with carbon quantum dots for removal of microplastics and antibiotics in aqueous environments
coredata.dc:creator: Nguyen, Manh B.
Doan, Huan V.
Le Hoang Tan, Doan
Lam, Tran Dai
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel approach was undertaken by integrating the photocatalyst graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with FeNi-BTC (BTC: Benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance the interaction between semiconductors. The g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material was synthesized using an environmentally friendly method that eschews the use of harmful solvents, using a combination of hydrothermal and microwave methods. The resulting material boasts a large pore volume (1.192 cm3g–1), a high surface area (1090 m2g–1), a small particle size (40–60 nm), and the capability to absorb visible light (2.27–2.52 eV). This material demonstrates remarkable efficiency in adsorbing microplastics, including polystyrene and polyethylene terephthalate (PET), at a concentration of 1200 mg L–1, achieving nearly 100% efficiency in just 45 minutes. Additionally, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material shows significant effectiveness in degrading tetracycline, with an efficiency of 98.28% after 90 minutes of exposure to visible light irradiation. The study extensively explores factors that influence the pollutant treatment process, including time, concentration, pH value, temperature and the water source. The process of adsorbing MP on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is mainly physical adsorption, following pseudo-second-order reaction kinetics, endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0), and the adsorption process occurs spontaneously (ΔG < 0). This comprehensive approach provides valuable insights into the potential of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material for treating pollutants in a range of aquatic environments, highlighting its versatility and effectiveness in environmental remediation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel approach was undertaken by integrating the photocatalyst graphite carbon nitride (g-C3N4) with FeNi-BTC (BTC: Benzene-1,3,5-tricarboxylic acid) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) to enhance the interaction between semiconductors. The g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material was synthesized using an environmentally friendly method that eschews the use of harmful solvents, using a combination of hydrothermal and microwave methods. The resulting material boasts a large pore volume (1.192 cm3g–1), a high surface area (1090 m2g–1), a small particle size (40–60 nm), and the capability to absorb visible light (2.27–2.52 eV). This material demonstrates remarkable efficiency in adsorbing microplastics, including polystyrene and polyethylene terephthalate (PET), at a concentration of 1200 mg L–1, achieving nearly 100% efficiency in just 45 minutes. Additionally, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material shows significant effectiveness in degrading tetracycline, with an efficiency of 98.28% after 90 minutes of exposure to visible light irradiation. The study extensively explores factors that influence the pollutant treatment process, including time, concentration, pH value, temperature and the water source. The process of adsorbing MP on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is mainly physical adsorption, following pseudo-second-order reaction kinetics, endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0), and the adsorption process occurs spontaneously (ΔG < 0). This comprehensive approach provides valuable insights into the potential of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material for treating pollutants in a range of aquatic environments, highlighting its versatility and effectiveness in environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Microplastics (< 5 mm in size) have permeated our drinking water, food, air, and even human tissues such as blood and lungs [1–3]. This widespread contamination is primarily due to microplastic pollution in the air, water sources, seas, and oceans, which negatively impacts countless marine organisms [4,5]. Annually, it's estimated that 19–23 million tons of plastic waste are released globally [6]. Projections indicate that by 2030, this figure could soar to 53 million tons per year, with approximately 10 million tons ending up in the oceans [6]. Despite the increasing accumulation of plastic waste, only about 11% is recycled [7]. The resilience of plastic waste means it can linger in the environment for centuries, posing a significant environmental threat [8–11]. Microplastics are particularly hazardous due to their small size and hydrophobic nature, enabling them to easily penetrate biological barriers such as the placenta, bloodstream, digestive tract, and lungs [1–3]. Once inside the body, microplastics can induce oxidative stress in cells, trigger infections, suppress the immune system, and disrupt endocrine functions [12,13]. Additionally, the large surface area and affinity for lipids characteristic of plastics allow them to accumulate bacteria, viruses, and other persistent organic pollutants such as antibiotics and dyes from contaminated environments, exacerbating their ecological impact [14,15]. Antibiotics, originating from human and animal urine, digestive systems, plants, and hospital wastewater, are a primary contributor to 700,000 annual deaths, a figure expected to surge to 10 million within the next 35 years [16]. Research by Rubin et al. has shown that plastics can increase the toxicity of antibiotic pollutants in the environment by up to ten times, as they can adsorb these pollutants [17].

In response to these risks, a variety of methods for extracting both microplastics and antibiotics from water sources have been researched [18]. These include coagulation, various forms of filtration, flotation, hydroxyl radical oxidation, magnetic separation, photocatalytic degradation, adsorption, and the use of membranes [19–23]. However, each method has its limitations. For example, standard filtration systems can typically only remove particles around 20 µm in size, leaving behind smaller microplastics (< 5 µm) and nanoparticles. Nanofiltration devices, while potentially more effective, must be fine-tuned for specific pollutants, have a slower filtration rate, are difficult to recover, require high pressure to operate, and consequently incur significantly higher costs [18]. Photocatalysis, while effective for degrading microplastics, is limited by its slow rate of microplastic removal and long processing times [24]. For example, only 65% of polypropylene (PP) particles measuring 150 µm were photodegraded over 19 days using nano ZnO rods [24]. Among the abovementioned methods, adsorption stands out for its cost-effectiveness and efficiency. This method operates at room temperature and offers high selectivity, rapid adsorption, and significant adsorption capacity [18,25–28].

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), known for their expansive surface areas (1000–2000 m² g–1) and adjustable pore sizes, have been increasingly utilized for microplastic adsorption in aquatic environments. For example, C@FeO/2D-MOF nanotubes were found to adsorb 100% of plastic waste in 60 minutes, maintaining over 90% efficiency after six cycles [18]. Similarly, Chen et al. [25] discovered that UiO-66-OH@MF (UiO-66: Universitetet i Oslo; MF: melamine foam) could remove up to 95.5 ± 1.2% of plastic waste. Wan et al. [26] reported that ZIF-67 achieved a plastic removal efficiency of 92.1% and reached adsorption equilibrium in just 20 min at 25 °C. Additionally, Modak et al. [27] found that MIL-101(Cr) material could remove 96% of plastic waste and had the highest adsorption capacity of 800 mg g–1. Chen et al. [28] developed TiO2/Ni-MOF material, deposited on the surface of polyurethane, which successfully absorbed 99% of microplastic and treated 95.1% of pesticides in high-salinity water. You et al. [29] designed ZIF-8@aerogel material capable of removing 85.8% of polystyrene, with a concentration of 500 mg L–1 and particle sizes between 90 and 140 nm. Additionally, Jibo Qin et al. synthesized an Ag2O/Fe-BDC (BDC: Benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid), which not only recycles plastic particles but also facilitates hydrogen production when exposed to light [30]. The remarkable efficiency of MOFs in treating plastic particles is largely attributed to electrostatic, hydrophilic, and π-π interactions between MOFs and microplastic particles [31].

In this research, we pioneered the integration of g-C3N4/FeNi-BTC materials with carbon quantum dots (CQD), achieving this synthesis without the use of toxic solvents. The resulting g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC materials are characterized by their small size (40–60 nm), high surface area, and robust stability under various environmental conditions. These materials demonstrate the capability to simultaneously address multiple pollutants, including microplastics (MP) and antibiotics. We have extensively studied and identified key factors that influence the treatment process of microplastics and antibiotics. These factors include the mass of the material used, the pH value, the concentration of pollutants, the source of water (including variability in seawater salinity), and the specific reaction mechanisms involved. Our analysis and conclusions are supported by data obtained from Liquid Chromatography-Mass Spectrometry (LC-MS) results. Given the prevalence of microplastics and antibiotics in aquatic environments, our study specifically focused on the effects of different water sources in Hanoi, Vietnam. This included an examination of various rivers and lakes such as the Tay Lake, Hoan Kiem Lake, Dong Mo Lake, Hong River, Nhue River, and To Lich River, as well as seawater. The insights gained from these investigations are critical in understanding and improving the efficacy of our synthesized materials in real-world conditions.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using chitosan as the precursor. Specifically, 1.0 g of chitosan was added to a 200 mL solution containing 1% CH3COOH and vigorously stirred to ensure homogeneity. This solution was then transferred into an autoclave and incubated for 12 h at 180 °C. The resultant yellowish-brown mixture, which contains the CQDs, was subsequently isolated using centrifugation. Once synthesized, the CQDs were preserved at 2 °C for further use in the next step.

The synthesis of the g-C3N4 material was conducted following the procedure outlined in our prior research [32]. Initially, a mixture comprising equal parts of urea and NH4Cl, each weighing 9 g, was prepared. This mixture was then placed in a ceramic crucible, which was covered with a melamine foam lid. The crucible was subsequently heated at a temperature of 550 °C for 2 hours. This process facilitated the formation of the g-C3N4 sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of g-C3N4/FeNi-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC and g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC materials
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of g-C3N4/FeNi-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC materials involved a combined hydrothermal and microwave irradiation method. For the g-C3N4/Fe8Ni2BTC (Fe: Ni molar ratio is 8:2), the process began by dissolving 12 mmol (2.52 g) of trimesic acid in 60 mL of 1 M NaOH to form a colorless solution (solution A). Concurrently, 9.6 mmol (2.595 g) of FeCl3.6 H2O and 2.4 mmol (0.571 g) of NiCl2.6 H2O were dissolved in 60 mL of water and sonicated to achieve a uniform solution (solution B). In parallel, 0.8 g of g-C3N4 was dispersed in 30 mL of water through 30 minutes of sonication at room temperature. The composition of g-C3N4: FeNiBTC phases by weight is calculated to be 0.20: 0.8 based on theoretical ratios. Next, solution B was gradually added to solution A and stirred for 30 minutes to form solution C. The g-C3N4 solution was then incorporated into solution C and stirred vigorously at 500 rpm for 30 minutes. This mixture was transferred to a Teflon flask and hydrothermally treated in a microwave at 100 °C for 30 minutes. After cooling to room temperature, the mixture was washed with ethanol and water to yield the g-C3N4/Fe8Ni2BTC material. Other variants, like g-C3N4/Fe9Ni1BTC, g-C3N4/Fe7Ni3BTC, g-C3N4/FeBTC, g-C3N4/NiBTC were synthesized following the same procedure (Figure S1). The ratios of the input materials, including metal salts and linker trimesic acid, are provided in Table S1.

For the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC materials, the process involved the integration of CQDs. Initially, 20 mL of the previously synthesized CQD solution was added to 100 mL of a water-ethanol mixture (volume ratio 4:1). Subsequently, 2 g of the g-C3N4/Fe8Ni2BTC sample was introduced into the CQD mixture, ultrasonicated for 30 minutes, and then stirred vigorously at 120 °C for 5 hours. The resulting mixture was separated by centrifugation, washed with a solvent, and the solid residue was dried at 80 °C for 12 hours to obtain the final g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material. CQD accounts for about 0.5 wt% as a connecting agent between g-C3N4 and FeNiBTC semiconductors of g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC materials.

In our experiment, 50 mg of each sample, g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC, were mixed into 20 mL of a microplastic (specifically polystyrene, 0.6 µm) suspension with a concentration of 1000 mg L−1. This mixture was then stirred at a speed of 500 rpm. After the necessary adsorption time, the sample was filtered using filter paper to recover the adsorbent and the microplastics absorbed on the material. The solid sample was then separated and dried at 100 °C for 12 hours, and the weight was measured after adsorption. The effectiveness of the removal microplastic particles is determined by the weight analysis method based on previous studies [18,29,33], according to equation 1.

where m1 is the weight of the adsorbent, m2 is the weight of the adsorbent after adsorbing microplastics, and m0 is the initial weight of microplastics in the solution (m0 = 50 mg, ưhich corresponds to 20 mL MP to a concentration of 1000 mg L–1).

Additionally, the water solution after removal of the adsorbent was tested using UV-Vis method with MP peaks (250–295 nm), and dynamic light scattering (DLS) was used to determine the microplastics [18,34].

For the recycling experiment, the procedure was as follows: After each adsorption cycle, the procedure was as follows: After each adsorption cycle, the material was soaked for 6 h with 10 mL of dichloromethane for 2 times to dissolve the microplastic, and this solid was then soaked for 6 h with 60 mL of ethanol for 2 times. Final, rinsing with deionized water and drying under vacuum for further experiments [18,28,35,36]. This washing and drying process was consistently replicated in subsequent cycles. Additionally, the weight of each material was recorded both before and after the adsorption process in each cycle. This data was used to calculate the treatment efficiency, providing insights into the sustainability and effectiveness of the materials over multiple cycles of use.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns and Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples are presented in Fig. 1. In Fig. 1A, the XRD pattern of the g-C3N4 sample shows a peak at 27.3° corresponding to the (002) reflection plane of the g-C3N4 phase [37–39]. The g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC sample exhibits peaks at 2θ of 7.28°, 10.93°, 12.71°, 13.20°, 14.73°, 16.84°, 18.37°, 19.50°, and 22.43°, which are characteristic of the Ni-BTC phase structure (CCDC: 802890) [40–42]. In the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC sample, peaks appear at 2θ of 6.20°, 10.36°, 11.08°, 12.55°, 13.92°, 19.02°, 20.16°, 24.12°, and 27.70°, which are characteristic of the Fe-BTC phase (CCDC: 640536) [43–45].

In the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples, the intensity of the characteristic Ni-BTC peak is very low, possibly due to the overlapping of the peaks of the Fe-BTC phase and the even dispersion of the Fe-BTC nanoparticles within the g-C3N4 plate. Additionally, no peak at 2θ of 27.62°, which is characteristic of the g-C3N4 structure, is observed in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples. This is because the layered structure of the g-C3N4 material helps to evenly disperse the M-BTC (Ni-BTC and Fe-BTC) nanoparticles on the surface of the g-C3N4 plate [37].

In Fig. 1B, the FT-IR result confirms the presence of –OH groups stretching vibrations adsorbed on the surface of MOFs and –NH groups in g-C3N4 in the wavelength range of 3100–3500 cm–1. The stretching CO bonds (1627 and 1575 cm–1), asymmetric (1450 and 1382 cm–1), phenyl ring C-H out-of-plane vibrations (759 and 710 cm–1), C–O–Me bonds (C-O-Fe and C-O-Ni, 1109 cm–1), iron-oxo cluster (622 cm–1) [39], and Ni–O bonds (1102, 675 and 571 cm–1) are observed [46,47]. Notably, the peak at 1102, 673 and 571 cm–1, characteristic of the Ni–O bond, increases as the Ni content in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples increases. Furthermore, the presence of sp2 C–H bonds (2923 cm–1) and sp2 C–H stretch (2866 cm–1) of CQD has demonstrated the successful integration of CQD with g-C3N4 and MOFs [48]. The FTIR results of all samples demonstrate that the structure and surface chemical properties of the composites are preserved and similar to those of the individual samples.

The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum and EDS mapping of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material is presented in Figure S2. This figure shows that the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material consists of C, O, N, Fe, and Ni. The presence of Ni, ranging from 1.74 to 5.63 wt%, confirms its incorporation in the MOF component, likely replacing some of the Fe in the secondary building units (SBUs) or inorganic nodes [49]. The EDS mapping image illustrates a uniform distribution of C, O, N, Fe, and Ni within the structure of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material. According to Table S2, the g-C3N4 material is predominantly made up of C (40.08 wt%) and N (59.92 wt%). The g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC material consists of C (46.23 wt%), N (14.26 wt%), Ni (15.63 wt%), and O (23.88 wt%). For the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples, there is an observable increase in the Ni content, which ranges from 1.74 to 5.63 wt%, while the Fe content decreases from 17.09 wt% to 12.58 wt%. These EDS and EDS-mapping findings confirm the successful synthesis of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material, achieved through a hydrothermal and microwave-assisted method. The even elemental distribution and the variation in metal content in the different samples highlight the efficacy of the synthesis process and the potential for tailoring the materials composition for specific applications. In Figure S3, the full-scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra of the g-C3N4, FeNi-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples confirm the characteristic peaks of all elements including C (284 eV), O (530 eV), N (399 eV), Fe (711 eV), and Ni (856 eV) [37]. These peaks confirm the presence of these elements in the respective samples.

For the g-C3N4 sample, the C 1 s XPS spectra can be deconvoluted into three distinct peaks, located at binding energies of 284.48, 285.33, and 287.99 eV. These peaks correspond to the C–C, C–N and N–CN bonds, respectively [50].

This spectrum reveals the bonding environment of carbon within the g-C3N4 structure. In the FeNi-BTC sample, the C1s XPS spectra exhibit peaks at 284.55, 286.22, 288.36, and 290.65 eV. These peaks are attributed to the bonds C–C/CC, C–O, CO and OC–O bonds, respectively [37]. These binding energies provide insight into the chemical composition and structure of the FeNi-BTC material. The N 1 s XPS spectra of the g-C3N4 sample have three distinct peaks at binding energies of 398.93, 399.98, and 401.35 eV. These peaks are assigned to different nitrogen environments including sp2N in C–NC bonds, tertiary sp3N nitrogen atoms, and C2–NH amine groups, respectively [51–53]. In the O 1 s XPS spectra of the FeNi-BTC sample, the observed binding energy peaks at 530.55, 531.46 eV, and 532.84 eV correspond to various oxygen-containing functional groups. Specifically, the OC–O group is identified at 530.55 eV, metal-oxygen bonds (Me–O) within the MOFs network (such as Fe–O or Ni–O) at 531.46 eV, and hydroxyl (–OH) groups adsorbed on the MOFs surface at 532.84 eV [54].

The high-resolution Ni 2p and Fe 2p XPS spectra of the FeNi-BTC sample reveal peaks characteristic of different oxidation states: Fe2+ (at 711.09 and 724.62 eV), Fe3+ (at 713.28 and 728.09 eV), Ni2+ (at 856.03 and 873.57 eV), and Ni3+ (at 859.58 and 878.33 eV). These peaks indicate the presence of iron and nickel in multiple oxidation states within the sample. For the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC sample, the XPS spectra show unique peaks at 708.91 eV and 853.62 eV, attributed to Fe–N and Ni–N bonds, respectively. This suggests a covalent interaction between the MOFs and g-C3N4, particularly through metal-nitrogen (Me–N) bonding. It is noteworthy that there are shifts in the characteristic binding energies in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC sample compared to the FeNi-BTC and g-C3N4 samples. In the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC sample, the binding energies of Fe2p, Ni2p, O1s, and C1s exhibit a positive energy shift compared to the FeNi-BTC sample. This shift is attributed to a decrease in electron density in the FeNi-BTC phase. Conversely, the binding energies of N1s and C1s in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC sample show a negative change due to increased electron count in the g-C3N4 phase (Table S3). The electron density of the g-C3N4 sample increases while that of FeNi-BTC decreases. Consequently, the anticipated electron transfer pathway in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC sample is from the FeNi-BTC phase to the g-C3N4 phase [55]. This charge density transfer occurs favorably as a result of the formation of Me–N bonds (Fe–N and Ni–N) and carbon quantum dots acting as bridges. These factors facilitate a robust bond between g-C3N4 and FeNi-BTC, creating conducive conditions for charge carrier movement. This movement leads to changes in the electron density of the elements, consequently altering the binding energy positions [56]. The formation of covalent bond between FeNi-BTC and g-C3N4 establishes favorable conditions for the creation of a heterojunction of the Z-scheme. This setup facilitates efficient photocatalytic activity by promoting effective transport and separation of charge carriers [57].

The nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms for all samples exhibit Type IV behavior according to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, featuring a hysteresis loop at the relative pressure of 0.9–1, as shown in Figure S5 [58]. This indicates the presence of mesoporous structures within the samples Fig. 2.

Table 1 shows the surface area and pore volume of g-C3N4, which are 87.2 m2 g–1 and 0.559 cm3 g–1, respectively. Meanwhile, the CQD material has surface area and pore volume of 2.2 m2 g–1 and 0.008 cm3 g–1, respectively. Upon the integration of Ni-BTC and Fe-BTC onto the surface of the g-C3N4 plate, there is a notable increase in surface area: 177 m2 g–1 for the Ni-BTC and 1031 m2 g–1 for the Fe-BTC modified samples. Although the Ni-BTC material initially has a lower surface area compared to Fe-BTC, the substitution of approximately 10% and 20% of Fe with Ni leads to a significant increase in both surface area and pore volume. However, further increasing the Ni content in the sample (from 20% to 30% compared to Fe) results in a reduction of these properties. For instance, the surface area and pore volume decrease to 924 m2 g–1 and 1.002 cm3 g–1, respectively, for the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3-BTC sample. This trend of decreasing surface area and pore volume with higher Ni content is also observed in other materials such as Co-Fe-BTC/g-C3N4 and Fe-Cu-BTC materials [37,59].

The pore diameter of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC and g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC materials were analyzed using the density functional theory (DFT) method. For the g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC sample, the pore structure comprises two distinct cage sizes, measuring 1.87 nm and 2.27 nm in diameter. In comparison, the g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC material has three different pore sizes: 1.9, 2.25 and 2.62 nm. The substitution of Ni2+ ions, which have an ionic radius of 0.69 Å, in place of some of the Fe3+ ions (with an ionic radius of 0.64 Å) in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC sample, leads to a slight increase in pore diameter. As a result, in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material, the pore diameters range between 1.87 – 1.90 nm and 2.27–2.38 nm. This indicates that the incorporation of Ni2+ ions into the framework not only modifies the metal composition but also subtly alters the pore structure, potentially impacting the material's adsorption and catalytic properties.

SEM image of g-C3N4 material shows sheet-like structures with sizes ranging from 300 to 500 nm (Figure S6A). SEM image of g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample reveals spherical FeNi-BTC nano particles, which are well distributed and coated along with g-C3N4 sheets (Figure S6B). These FeNi-BTC nanoparticles provide uniform shielding and coating on the g-C3N4 sheets, resulting in fewer high-energy electron clusters passing through the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC samples compared to individual g-C3N4 samples, thus creating darker regions in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC samples. This makes it difficult to observe the g-C3N4 sheets in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC samples ( Fig. 3).

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the g-C3N4 material reveals plate-like structures with an average size of around 300 nm (Fig. 3). Notably, these plates appear brighter and thinner compared to those observed in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC nanocomposite. For the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples, the TEM images show the presence of much smaller particles, with their sizes predominantly ranging from 20 to 40 nm. This size reduction indicates a significant change in the morphology of the material after the incorporation of MOFs and CQDs. The combined analysis from XRD, XPS, and TEM results confirms the successful synthesis and integration of MOFs onto the g-C3N4 plates, facilitated by the CQD bridge. The formation of O–Fe–N and O–Ni–N bonds is a critical factor in this successful attachment, signifying the creation of a hybrid material that combines the properties of both MOFs and g-C3N4. This hybridization is likely to enhance the material's performance in applications such as catalysis and adsorption due to the synergistic effects of the combined components.

The activity of the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, and different compositions of g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC materials was assessed based on their ability to treat polystyrene microplastic particles with a diameter of 0.6 µm. The investigation into the adsorption efficiency for microplastic (MP) removal revealed varying degrees of effectiveness among the different samples. Specifically, the g-C3N4 sample exhibited an adsorption efficiency of 20.37%, indicating a moderate level of effectiveness.

The g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC sample showed a significantly higher efficiency at 38.36%. A remarkable improvement was observed with the g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC sample, which achieved an adsorption efficiency of 90.88% ( Fig. 4A). Further enhancements were noted in the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples with varying ratios of Fe to Ni. The g-C3N4/CQD/Fe9Ni1BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC samples reached adsorption efficiencies of 97.53%, 99.32%, and 92.63%, respectively. These results, illustrated in Fig. 4A, demonstrate the significant impact of integrating Fe and Ni with g-C3N4/CQD in enhancing the adsorption of microplastics, with the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC composition showing the highest efficiency. The low plastic removal efficiency of the g-C3N4 and g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC samples can be attributed to their relatively small surface areas, as detailed in Table 1.

In contrast, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material demonstrated the highest MP treatment efficiency at 99.32%, likely owing to its superior surface area and porous volume. Interestingly, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample outperforms the individual g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC and g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC samples in MP treatment efficiency. This improved performance can be attributed to the synergistic effect of combining both metal ions (Fe and Ni) within the same basic network structure (SBU). However, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC sample shows a reduced MP adsorption efficiency of 92.63% compared to the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample. This decrease is likely due to the excessive increase in Ni content, which leads to a substantial reduction in both volume and surface area, consequently diminishing the MP treatment efficiency.

The high adsorption efficiency (over 90%) of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples can be credited to the strong interaction between the aromatic ring structure of the MOFs and the MPs. This interaction facilitates the formation of π-π covalent bonds and stronger electrostatic attraction of MOFs [21]. Here, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material, with the highest MP removal efficiency, was selected for further study to understand the factors affecting the MP adsorption process. Various factors influencing the efficiency of plastic removal such as time, pH value, and plastic concentration have been studied. Fig. 4B shows that adsorption time significantly impacts MP removal efficiency in water. Specifically, increasing the MP adsorption time from 15 minutes to 45 minutes enhances the MP removal efficiency from 70.34% to 99.32%. However, extending the adsorption time further, from 45 min to 120 min, does not result in a significant change in efficiency (remaining over 99%), as the process reaches adsorption equilibrium.

Figure S7 shows the influence of pH values on the surface charge of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material. The isoelectric point (IEP) of g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC is 7.5, so at pH values less than 7.5, the material is positively charged, while at pH values greater than 7.5, it is negatively charged. Fig. 4C demonstrates that the pH value significantly influences the MP removal rate in water. For the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material, the MP removal efficiency exceeds 99% in acidic and neutral conditions (pH < 7.5). This high efficiency can be attributed to the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material and the negatively charged MP particles [60]. Conversely, in alkaline environments with high pH values (pH > 7.5), both the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material and the MP particles acquire a negative charge. This similarity in charge leads to a repulsion between them, which adversely affects the adsorption process. Consequently, the MP removal efficiency is reduced in such conditions, dropping to 78.64% at a pH of 9. This observation underlines the importance of pH in the adsorption process and its role in influencing the efficiency of MP removal by the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material. Therefore, pH affects the adsorption process of MP, indicating that electrostatic attraction plays an important role in the adsorption mechanism of g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material [61].

Fig. 4D confirms that the initial concentration of MP affects the efficiency of MP treatment using the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC adsorbent. The data indicates an inverse relationship between the initial concentration of microplastics and the efficiency of MP treatment. Specifically, as the concentration of microplastics is reduced from 2500 to 1200 mg L–1, the efficiency of microplastic treatment improves significantly, rising from 48.65% to 99.68% after a 45-minute adsorption period. Remarkably, at concentrations ranging from 100 to 1200 mg L–1, the material achieves 100% removal of microplastics within the same duration. These findings highlight the high adsorption capacity of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material, estimated to be around 600 mg g–1, under conditions of 25 °C and atmospheric pressure. This suggests that the material is particularly effective at lower concentrations of microplastics.

Fig. 4E further demonstrates that the presence of various anions (Cl–, HCO3 –, and SO4 2–) at a concentration of 1 mmol L–1 exerts minimal influence on the microplastics treatment efficiency, with adsorption efficiencies exceeding 98%. This indicates that these anions do not significantly alter the reaction environment at this concentration. However, Fig. 4F shows a notable change when the concentration of these anions is increased to 10 mmol L–1. At this higher concentration, the efficiency of microplastic adsorption is substantially impacted. This effect can be attributed to the altered reaction environment caused by the anions, particularly their influence on increasing the pH. The change in pH due to the higher anion concentration affects the interaction dynamics between the adsorbent and the microplastics, leading to a decrease in adsorption efficiency.

Figure S8A shows the appearance of the original microplastic sample at a concentration of 1200 mg L–1, characterized by a milky white color with white flocculation suspended in water. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis of the MP (Figure S8B) reveals a high intensity, with the microparticle size measured at approximately 0.6 µm. This result confirms the presence of microplastics in the water sample. After a 45-minute adsorption process using the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material, there is a remarkable change in the water’s appearance. The solution becomes transparent, and the intensity of the DLS spectrum drops to zero, indicating the complete removal of plastic from the water. Furthermore, the UV-Vis spectroscopy analysis (Figure S8C) shows a notable peak in the 250–280 nm range for the untreated plastic sample. This peak is consistent with findings reported by Haris et al. [18]. This spectral feature, however, completely vanishes after 45 minutes of adsorption by the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material. The resulting UV-Vis spectrum closely aligns with the Ox axis from 800 to 220 nm, showing a distinct difference from the original plastic sample. These observations collectively demonstrate that the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material effectively removes 100% of the microplastics from the water source within a 45-minute adsorption period.

Table 2 presents data affirming the superior adsorption efficiency of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material for MP compared to previous studies. This material exhibits an impressive capability, successfully adsorbing microplastics at a concentration of 600 mg L–1 with 100% efficiency in just 45 min. This performance is noteworthy not only for its high efficiency but also for the relatively short adsorption time and the lower amount of adsorbent material required. These results underscore the high potential of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material in environmental remediation, particularly for the removal of microplastics from water environments. Its effectiveness, combined with the efficiency of the process, positions this material as a promising solution for addressing the growing concern of microplastic pollution in aquatic ecosystems.

The diffusion rate and adsorption capacity of MP are affected by the reaction temperature. Fig. 5 shows that as the temperature increases from 15 to 35 °C, the adsorption efficiency of g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material increases from 74.92% to 99.6% after 45 min of MP adsorption. The increase in temperature accelerates the diffusion rate and the speed of movement of the participating substances in the reaction [36]. However, when the reaction temperature increases to 40 °C, the removal efficiency of the simulated microplastic decreases from 99.6% to 88.36%. This reduction is due to the weakened electrostatic bonding force between the material and the MP, leading to decreased adsorption capacity [32,36]. This result is consistent with the observation reported by Xu and Verma [32,36].

First-order and pseudo-second-order kinetic models were used to assume the adsorption kinetics of MP on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material. Figure S9 and Table S4 shows that the pseudo-second-order kinetic data (R2 = 0.991) provides a better fit than the pseudo-first-order kinetic data (R2 = 0.946). This observation is in line with the findings of Haris [18] and Xu [36]. Functional groups such as –COOH, CN, –NH2, –OH act as adsorption sites, capable of forming π-π conjugation and electrostatic interactions [36]. The rate constants for MP adsorption in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material at temperatures of 15, 20, 25, 30, and 35 °C are 1.054 ×10–4, 1.748 ×10–4, 2.445 ×10–4, 2.711 ×10–4, 2.921 ×10–4, respectively.

Various isotherm models, including Freundlich, Langmuir, Jovanovic, and Khan, were used to analyze the experimental data of MP adsorption equilibrium on the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material at different temperatures. Nonlinear optimization techniques were utilized to minimize the error function in these isotherm models ( Fig. 6). Table 3 and Table S5 shows that the Langmuir model yields a higher R2 value compared to the Freundlich, Jovanovic, and Khan models. This suggests that the MP adsorption process on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is more accurately described by the Langmuir model, confirming that this adsorption is a monolayer process. This finding is consistent with the reports of Xu [36] and Gao [33]. The equilibrium adsorption capacities (qe) of MP at temperatures of 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 °C, as determined by the Langmuir model, are 478.73, 482.55, 490.56, 500.13, 507.08, and 492.84 mg/g, respectively. Furthermore, Table 3 shows that the separation factor (RL) for the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material falls within the range of 0–1, and the exponent (1/n) is less than 1. These values suggest that the shape of the isotherm is concave, indicating favorable conditions for adsorption [61].

The thermodynamics of the MP adsorption was studied to gain a deeper understanding of the standard entropy change (ΔS), standard enthalpy change (ΔH), and Gibbs free energy change (ΔG). These parameters were calculated using the following Eqs. 2 and 3:
(2) ΔG = RTlnKC
(3)
ln K C
=
Δ S
R
−
Δ H
RT
where, R is the ideal gas constant with a value of 8.314 J/mol, and T (K) is the absolute temperature.

The equilibrium constant value (KC) is calculated as the Langmuir constant (KL), given that the Langmuir isotherm best describes the MP adsorption.
KC = 1000 x KL x MMP x 55.5

In which, the molecular weight of the adsorbent (MMP) is 104.15 g mol–1, 55.5 mol L–1 is the number of moles of pure water per liter, and the conversion units 1000 is used to convert g mol–1 to mg mol–1 [61,63].

The thermodynamic parameters (ΔG, ΔH and ΔS) for the MP adsorption on the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material are presented in Table 4. The relationship between ΔG, ΔH, and ΔS is determined based on the slope and intersection of the lnKC graph plotted against 1/T (Figure S11). The thermodynamic parameters for the adsorption of MP on the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material are presented in Table 4. In this table, all ΔG values across the investigated temperatures are negative, indicating that the adsorption of MP onto the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is favorable. This observation aligns with the separation factor values (0 < RL < 1) and the Freundlich model exponent in Table 3. Both the KC and ΔG values for the MP adsorption process increase with rising reaction temperatures, ranging from 15 to 35 °C. This trend suggests that the adsorption become more thermodynamically favorable at elevated temperatures.

The positive value of ΔH° (12.58 kJ mol–1) indicates that the adsorption of MP onto the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is an endothermic reaction. In other words, higher temperatures provide favorable thermodynammic conditions. The value of ΔH = 12.58 KJ mol–1, which is lower than the enthalpy change required for the chemical adsorption process (ΔH > 80 KJ mol−1), indicating that the adsorption process of MP on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC is mainly physical adsorption. This finding is consistent with the observed increase in adsorption capacity (qe) and equilibrium constant (KC) as shown Table 3 and Table 4. Additionally, the positve value of ΔS (173.43 J/mol) suggests that the adsorption process of MP is entropy-driven rather than enthalpy-driven [61].

The g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2-BTC material was evaluated for its stability and reusability across various microplastic adsorption cycles (Fig. 4I). Remarkably, after six adsorption cycles, the material maintained over 95% efficiency in removing microplastics from water. A minor decrease in microplastic adsorption efficiency was noted, which correlated with a slight reduction in surface area from 1037 to 884 m2 g–1 (Figure S12). The XRD patterns (Figure S13) and TEM images (Figure S14) provide insights into the structural integrity of the material. These analyses reveal that the material's structure, morphology, and phase structure remain largely unchanged before and after the reaction, indicating a high degree of stability. The observed slight decrease in microplastic adsorption efficiency in the sixth cycle could be attributed to the blocking of active sites due to the of contaminants. This suggests that while the material continues to perform effectively, there may be a gradual accumulation of substances on its surface over multiple cycles, which slightly impairs its adsorption capacity.

Overall, these results demonstrate that the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC material possesses significant durability. This durability is a consequence of the successful integration of g-C3N4 and FeNiBTC with CQDs, which enhances its stability and reusability in microplastic adsorption applications. The material's sustained high performance over multiple cycles showcases its potential for long-term use in environmental remediation efforts, particularly in treating microplastic pollution in water sources.

Fig. 7 illustrates the electrical and optical properties of the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples. As shown in Fig. 7A, the g-C3N4 sample exhibits strong light absorption in the wavelength range below 450 nm. This indicates its ability to absorb both visible and ultraviolet light. The g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC sample also demonstrates strong absorption in the visible and ultraviolet light spectra. Notably, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples exhibit an increased intensity of absorption within the visible light range. This enhanced absorption in the visible spectrum could be attributed to the synergistic effect of the metal ions located in the secondary building units (SBU) of the material.

Additionally, the observed shift in the light absorption range of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples may be linked to the varying electronic configurations of the metal ions at the network nodes. These differences in electronic structure can lead to alterations in the light absorption characteristics of the material [64]. Furthermore, the incorporation of CQDs is believed to play a pivotal role in augmenting the interaction between semiconductor materials. The presence of CQDs enhances the diffusion, separation, and transport of electrons, contributing to more efficient light absorption and utilization in photocatalytic processes [65–67].

The band-gap energies (Eg) of all materials are calculated based on the Tauc plot equation, as illustrated in Figure S15. The Tauc plot method involves extrapolating the linear portion of the (αhv)2 versus hv plot (where α is the absorption coefficient and hv is the photon energy) to the energy axis (hv) at α = 0, thus estimating the band-gap energy. In Figure S15, the calculated Eg values for the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe9Ni1BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC samples are 2.73, 2.60, 2.52, 2.44, 2.34, and 2.27 eV, respectively. It can be seen that increasing the Ni content leads to a reduction in the Eg of the g-C3N4/CQD/NiFeBTC materials. Therefore, the Eg values for the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples range from 2.52 to 2.34 eV, making them more suitable for light absorption and beneficial for the photocatalytic treatment of pollutants.

One of the major challenges in photocatalysis is the rapid recombination of electron-hole pairs (e– and h+). Fig. 7B shows that the g-C3N4 material exhibits high emission intensity at a wavelength of 455 nm, which is characteristic of the n-π* electron transition and the recombination of e– and h+ [37]. The highest radiation intensity of g-C3N4 material confirms that this material has a high recombination rate between electrons and holes [68]. The attachment of MOFs to the surface of g-C3N4 alters the surface charge and valence of the material. This change leads to the formation of surface defects and charge defects, significantly reducing the luminescence intensity of g-C3N4, confirming the decrease in the recombination rate of electrons and holes. In addition, MOFs with a high surface area can trap electrons generated during the excitation process, thus creating electron deficiencies on the surface and reducing the recombination of e– and h+ [54]. The emission intensity of the material decreases in the following order: g-C3N4 > g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC > g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC > g-C3N4/CQD/Fe9Ni1BTC > g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC > g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC. Therefore, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material exhibits the lowest charge carrier recombination, indicating its potential as an effective photocatalytic material.

Charge separation and transmission characteristics of the g-C3N4, FeNi-BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples were determined by transient photocurrent spectroscopy (TPCR) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). TPCR was determined in 6 different on-off cycles with a time range from 0 to 360 seconds (Fig. 7C). The g-C3N4 and FeNiBTC materials had low photocurrent density, with a photocurrent intensity of 0.059 and 0.067 mA/cm2, respectively. After being combined with g-C3N4 sheets with FeNiBTC, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples had higher photocurrent density, indicating a high charge separation capability compared to individual samples. This result may be due to the synergistic effect of MOFs combined with g-C3N4 sheets and enhanced light absorption [56], [37], [69]. Furthermore, the formation of bridges through CQD and Me–N bonds (Fe–N and Ni–N) also allows electrons to easily move from the FeNiBTC phase to g-C3N4, leading to more efficient charge transmission and charge separation [69]. This result is consistent with the theoretical and experimental reports by Ma et al. [70]. The g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material had the highest photocurrent density, indicating the most efficient separation of electron-hole pairs.

The transmission of electric charge on the surface of materials is crucial as it directly influences the rate of electron/hole separation and the formation of reactive radicals. Fig. 7D shows that g-C3N4 has a significantly large Nyquist plot radius, approximately 3 KΩ, indicating poor electrical conductivity on the surface of the catalyst. This is attributed to g-C3N4 being a non-metallic photocatalyst. To enhance conductivity, MOFs have been efficiently attached to the surface of the g-C3N4 plates using a combination of microwave and hydrothermal methods. The incorporation of MOFs significantly reduces the Nyquist plot radius of g-C3N4, from 4 to 0.25 KΩ, notably in the case of FeBTC attachment. Furthermore, the presence of M2+ and M3+ ions in the g-C3N4/CQD/MOF samples has been found to improve conductivity [37]. This improvement is also echoed in the work of Ngan et al. for Fe-BTC materials, where Fe exists in both Fe2+ and Fe3+ valence states, demonstrating its application as an antibiotic sensor in water environments [71]. Therefore, electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements confirm that the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC photocatalyst has the ability to absorb visible light, reduce the recombination rate between electrons and holes, and demonstrate efficient electron separation and charge transfer, as evidenced by UV-Vis DRS, PL, TPRC, and EIS methods [72,73].

In this study, the performance of g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/Ni-BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC materials was evaluated for treating both microplastic and tetracycline antibiotics. For the microplastic treatment, after 3 days of visible light irradiation, the degradation efficiencies of MP (2500 mg L–1) was as follows: 26.53% for g-C3N4, 72.68% for g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, 78.46% for g-C3N4/CQD/Fe9Ni1BTC, 82.16% for g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC, 76.08% for g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC and 38.41% for g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC ( Fig. 8A). The adsorption efficiencies for TC of these samples varied between 21.83% and 53.04% after 60 min, as illustrated in Fig. 8B. During the photocatalytic process, with 90 minutes of light irradiation, the TC degradation efficiencies were observed to be 63.18%, 90.43%, 93.54%, 98.28%, 93.75%, and 86.26% for the g-C3N4, g-C3N4/CQD/FeBTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe9Ni1BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC, g-C3N4/CQD/Fe7Ni3BTC, and g-C3N4/CQD/NiBTC samples, respectively.

The g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC samples exhibited superior TC degradation compared to the g-C3N4 samples. This enhanced performance is attributed to the integration of active phases through the CQD bridge, which improved charge separation, reduced electron-hole recombination, and facilitated the movement of charge carriers. These findings were corroborated by UV-Vis DRS, PL, and EIS methods, as shown in Fig. 7. Therefore, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC samples are effective in treating both microplastic and antibiotics in water through adsorption and photocatalysis processes. The optimal conditions for TC removal using the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC photocatalyst resulted in a remarkable degradation efficiency of 98.28% after 90 minutes of visible light irradiation. This was achieved with a TC concentration of 30 mg L–1, a photocatalyst dosage of 0.3 g L–1, and at a pH of 6.

Under optimal conditions, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC photocatalyst demonstrates its remarkable capability in removing various organic substances from aqueous media. This includes dyes such as reactive red 195 (RR-195), reactive yellow 145 (RY-145), methylene blue (MB), and rhodamine B (RhB), with an efficiency exceeding 97% after just 35 min of visible light irradiation (Figure S16). This high efficiency underscores the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material's potential as a versatile and effective treatment solution for organic pollutants and antibiotics in water.

Comparative data presented in Table S6 outlines the performance of different materials, such as UiO-66-NDC/PC3N4, g-C3N4/UU-200, and β-Bi2O3/Ag2O in treating TC in water environments. The reported removal efficiencies for TC with these materials range from 72.6% to 92.4%, achieved over reaction times of 120–180 min. In this study, under optimal conditions ([TC] = 30 mg L–1, [photocatalyst dosage] = 0.25 g L–1, pH = 6, irradiation power of 60 W), the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material achieved 98.28% of TC removal after 90 min of visible light irradiation. The comparison in Table S6 highlights that the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC photocatalyst not only requires a lower photocatalyst dosage and less irradiation power but also achieves significantly higher TC removal efficiency compared to the materials in the referenced studies. This suggests that the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC material offers an efficient, less resource-intensive alternative for the treatment of TC and potentially other organic pollutants in water environments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of MP adsorption and photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of MP show that the polystyrene particles are spherical and exhibit a highly uniform size distribution, with each particle being approximately 0.6 µm in diameter, as shown in Figure S17 A and B. After treatment with the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC sample, the SEM images display a noticeable adherence of spherical MOF nanoparticles, about 50 nm in size, to the surface of these smooth spherical microplastic particles. The FTIR spectrum of MP (Figure S18) exhibits several characteristic vibration bands. These include the –OH group at 3430 cm–1, aliphatic C–H stretching vibrations in the range of 2800 –3050 cm–1, C–C bond stretches at 1602 and 1580 cm–1), aromatic ring stretches at 1496 and 1450 cm–1, in-plane C–H vibrations at 1068 and 1026 cm–1, out-of-plane phenyl ring at 756 and 695 cm–1, and C–O vibrations at 1162 cm–1 [60,62,74]. These features are consistent with the typical structure of polystyrene. Following the adsorption of microplastic, the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material exhibits new characteristic vibration bands around 2800 –3100 cm–1, which are attributed to the aliphatic C–H groups. This change indicates the successful adsorption of microplastic within the material's pores [75]. Additionally, other vibration bands of the plastic may overlap with those inherent to the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material, suggesting a potential interaction or bonding between the microplastic and the photocatalyst material. These observations demonstrate the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material's effectiveness in capturing microplastic particles from an aqueous environment.

The XPS spectrum of the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample, post-treatment of a pollutant mixture comprising MP and TC, shows no significant changes in the high-resolution C1s, O1s, N1s, Fe 2p, and Ni2p XPS spectra before and after reaction (Fig. 2). However, there is a notable shift in the composition of characteristic C1s bonds, indicative of strong MP adsorption. Specifically, the proportion of the C–C/CC bond in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample increases from 45.18% to 61.25%, and the C–O bond escalates from 6.88% to 9.55% after MP adsorption. This change suggests oxidation or ageing of the MP, leading to the formation of C–O bonds in the microplastic structure [18,76,77]. Additionally, the π-π* bond of the phenyl rings in the plastic, with a binding energy of 290.99 eV, overlaps with the bond energy of the OC–O groups. This results in a significant increase in the bond energy at 290.99 eV, from 2.22% to 5.12%, following plastic adsorption in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC sample [18]. These observations are consistent with the findings from SEM images and FTIR spectra (Figure S17-18).

The g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC materials also effectively adsorb tetracycline through the formation of π-π covalent bonds between the benzene rings of the antibiotics and MOFs. A unique aspect of this study is the degradation of adsorbed pollutants into less toxic substances via reactive species such as •O2 –, •OH and h+ (Figure S19). Upon stimulation of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC semiconductor material with energy greater than its bandgap energy (Eg), electrons (e–) from the VB move to the CB, leaving behind holes (h+). The electrons in FeNi-BTC then transfer to the VB of g-C3N4 via the CQD bridge and recombine with the h+, getting excited back to the CB of g-C3N4, thereby increasing the electron density of gC3N4 (Eqs. 2 and 3).
(2) g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC → g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC (e–/h+)
(3) FeNiBTC (e–) + g-C3N4 (h+) → Photons and Heart

In the CB of FeNi-BTC, the holes (h+) with energy (2.56 eV) higher than the potential of the H2O/•OH reaction (2.4 eV) react with water or hydroxyl groups on the photocatalyst surface to form •OH groups (see Eq. 4).
(4) h+ + H2O → h+ + •OH

At the same time, electrons in the VB of g-C3N4, with energy (–0.75 eV) lower than the standard potential of •O2–/O2 (–0.33 eV), react with O2 to form superoxide anion radicals (•O2–). Finally, these holes h+ and reactive species (•O2–, •OH) interact with MP and TC to transform them into less polluting substances, potentially mineralizing into CO2 and H2O [6].

It is noted that the amount of solid residue in the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe-NiBTC sample post-adsorption with microplastics is less than that before adsorption, which aligns with a decreased Fe content as verified by EDS results (Supplementary Information, Table S2). Post-reaction, there is a notable decrease in the wt% composition of Fe, Ni and N elements in the g-C3N4/Fe8Ni2BTC sample. Conversely, there is a significant increase in the wt% of carbon (from 44.96% to 56.15%) and oxygen (from 14.96% to 16.82%). This shift in composition is indicative of the material's high capacity to adsorb microplastics, which primarily consist of carbon and oxygen elements.

Intermediate products of the MP degradation process were collected by LC-MS method ( Fig. 9 and Table S7). The reaction radicals •O2 –, •OH formed will attack the unstable positions of MP, decomposing it into smaller molecular products and the final products are CO2 and H2O [24]. Specifically, the reaction radicals •O2 –, •OH attack the tertiary carbon positions and cleave the MP chain to form products T1 (m/z 224.22), T2 (m/z 148.81), T3 (m/z 94.90), T4 (m/z 120.62), T5 (m/z 136.03), T6 (m/z 110.12), and T7 (m/z 121.43) [78]. Products T1-T7 continue to react with the reaction radicals through ring-opening reactions, oxidation to form shorter chain products such as succinic acid (T8, m/z 118.14), T9 (m/z 84.86), propionic acid (T9, m/z 74.33), acetic acid (T10, m/z 60.12), and finally oxidized into inorganic products CO2 and H2O. The mineralization ability of MP is evaluated by total organic carbon (TOC), biological oxygen demand (BOD), and chemical oxygen demand (COD) methods. After 3 days of photocatalytic degradation of MP, the TOC, BOD, and COD values of the treated PS on the material reached 62.15, 73.88, and 76.21, respectively (Figure S21A). This result demonstrates the effective degradation of MP by the catalyst, but due to the large size of microplastic particles (0.6 µm), the degradation reaction time needs to be extended. These results are similar to previous studies on TiO2 and ZnO materials in the degradation process of polypropylene, achieving a degradation efficiency of 65% after 456 h [24]. After 90 min of visible light irradiation in the presence of g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2-BTC, the chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD) and total organic carbon (TOC) reached 77.62, 75.38 and 55.36%, respectively (Figure S21B). These results demonstrate that g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2-BTC material exhibits a high mineralization capacity for tetracycline under visible light conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluate the effectiveness of microplastic treatment in actual wastewater sources
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we investigated the effectiveness of microplastic treatment in various water sources from Hanoi, Vietnam, including the Hong River, Nhue River, To Lich River, Hoan Kiem Lake, Tay Lake, and Dong Mo Lake. The collected water samples were first filtered through a 0.22 µm filter paper to remove solid waste and then mixed with microplastics (polystyrene) at a concentration of 1000 mg L–1 to test the adsorption capability of the gC3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material (Fig. 4G).

Fig. 4G reveals significant variability in microplastic treatment efficiency across different water sources in Hanoi. This variability is attributed to the diverse pollutants present in these water bodies. In the To Lich River and Nhue River, the microplastic adsorption efficiency reached 52.63% and 64.96%, respectively, after 45 minutes. The lower efficiency in these rivers is likely due to the competition between microplastics and other organic and inorganic pollutants, as well as suspended particles [79,80].

Furthermore, considering the prevalence of microplastics in the ocean, the study also explored the impact of seawater salinity on microplastic treatment. Seawater collected from Quynh Luu, Nghe An, Vietnam, served as a test medium. Fig. 4H illustrates that after 45 minutes of treatment, high removal rates were achieved for various substances in seawater: 95.19% for polystyrene, 94.13% for polyethylene terephthalate (PET), 92.64% for tetracycline (TC), 91.88% for reactive red 195 (RR-195), as shown in Fig. 4H. These results demonstrate the robust ability of g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC material to effectively remove microplastics in real-world environmental conditions, including both freshwater and seawater contexts. This highlights its potential as a versatile solution for addressing microplastic pollution in diverse aquatic ecosystems.

In this study, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC materials were synthesized using an eco-friendly approach that avoids harmful solvents, through a hydrothermal method assisted by microwave irradiation. The g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC material is characterized by its ability to absorb visible light (with band-gap energies of 2.27–2.52 eV), small particle size (40–60 nm), high surface area (1090 m2 g–1), and large pore volume (1.192 cm3 g–1).

This material demonstrates exceptional adsorption capabilities, successfully capturing plastic particles (microplastics such as polystyrene and PET) at a concentration of 1200 mg L–1 with nearly 100% efficiency after 45 min, and effectively removing 98.28% of the antibiotic tetracycline from water. After 3 days of visible light irradiation, the degradation efficiency, TOC, BOD, and COD of MP (2500 mg L–1) on the g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8NiBTC photocatalyst reached 82.16, 62.15, 73.88, and 76.21%, respectively. The study also reveals that factors such as concentration, pH value, salinity, temperature and adsorption time significantly influence the efficiency of plastic adsorption. The high efficiency of the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNiBTC material in adsorbing plastics is due to electrostatic interactions, affinity to water, and π-π interactions between the components of MOF and microplastic particles. The process of adsorbing MP on g-C3N4/CQD/Fe8Ni2BTC material is mainly physical adsorption, following pseudo-second-order reaction kinetics, endothermic reaction (ΔH > 0), and the adsorption process occurs spontaneously (ΔG < 0).

Additionally, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC photocatalyst functions effectively as a direct Z-scheme photocatalyst to degrade antibiotics and dyes such as RR-195, RY-145, MB, and RhB. Furthermore, the g-C3N4/CQD/FeNi-BTC materials exhibit high stability, maintaining an adsorption efficiency of over 95% even after six cycles of microplastic adsorption. This durability is attributed to the strong binding of CQDs with active phases and the Z-scheme electron transfer mechanism, which helps protect the materials higher energy bandgap.

The study further extends its investigation to real-world water environments, including the Nhue River, To Lich River, Song River, Hoan Kiem Lake, Tay Lake, Dong Mo Lake in Hanoi, and seawater from Quynh Luu, Nghe An. These environments were used to evaluate the material's ability to remove plastic particles (1200 mg L–1) and tetracycline antibiotics (30 mg L–1), showcasing its potential for widespread environmental applications in various aquatic ecosystems.
